data.title: AGGH: Bonds, Hedges, And Capturing The Slope
data.description:
Introduction

In today's article, we will analyze the Simplify Aggregate Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:AGGH), managed by the investment firm Simplify. This vehicle seeks to occupy the ‘heart’ of the fixed income portfolio, but with a twist. Instead of simply passively replicating the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index, it applies active management and seeks to maximize total return while maintaining a profile similar to its benchmark index, adding extra income through tools such as efficient option writing and curve positioning. In other words, it retains the backbone of ‘Agg’ funds (Treasury bonds, investment-grade corporate bonds, MBS/ABS/CMBS), but seeks to extract more returns by managing duration, curve, and risk premiums with derivatives.

The fund managers build the core exposure to investment-grade fixed income by purchasing low-cost index ETFs and using Treasury futures to calibrate the target duration. In doing so, the management team seeks to ensure that, on average, the fund's risk (duration and credit quality) is similar to that of the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate. They also use financial options to control effective duration.

On 06/30/2025, for example, the fund's duration was 8.65 years, a useful reference for readers who want to gauge their sensitivity to the yield curve. In addition, the management team has stated that it overweights bonds with the best risk-adjusted returns within the IG universe, seeking to increase the core yield without structurally increasing credit risk.

The differential part of the management is the derivatives layer. The ETF can devote up to 20% of the portfolio (measured by purchase price in options or collateral in other derivatives) to two uses: (1) hedging risks (interest rates and credit) and (2) generating additional income. In the income strategy, the fund sells put and/or call spreads on fixed income instruments (e.g., bond indices or ETFs).

Readers may wonder why the fund sells spreads rather than ‘naked’ options. This is because spreads limit risk in exchange for a recurring premium; if the market moves ‘within expectations’, those options expire worthless and the premium remains as income. In addition, the manager can use options as ‘smart limit orders’, i.e., to structure bond purchases/sales when target prices are reached.

In parallel, for its hedging strategy, the fund uses futures, swaps, and options on interest rates, credit, and, even opportunistically, derivatives on equities, volatility, commodities, or currencies if they offer a more cost-efficient hedge for the risk of concern at that time. To structure some of these derivatives in a tax-efficient manner, the ETF may invest up to 25% in a subsidiary in the Cayman Islands and may also use reverse repos to finance positions when carry/collateral numbers are favourable.

The vehicle analyzed generates returns in the following way. First, like any vehicle in the ‘AGG’ category, it generates returns through the coupon and the revaluation of the bonds if interest rates fall (or if credit tightens). Second, it adds the option premium from the spreads it sells. This premium acts as an ‘extra’ yield that does not depend directly on the coupon but rather on implied volatility and where the strikes are placed. Third, with its positioning on the yield curve (for example, using Treasury futures and interest rate swaps), it can attempt to capture a roll-down or move to sections of the curve with a better risk-return ratio.

Analysis – Macro Fundamental

In this section, we will analyze some macroeconomic indicators in detail to try to offer the reader an accurate perspective and a sound recommendation on the vehicle in question.

The first metric to analyze will be the slope of the T-Note segment between 10-year and 7-year notes. This chart classifies the slope of the ‘belly-long’ section of the yield curve according to four regimes: bull steep (orange: the curve steepens as rates fall), bear steep (green: steepens as rates rise), bear flat (blue: flattens as rates rise), and bull flat (purple: flattens as rates fall).

Since mid-2024, and especially in 2025, the indicator has entered a sustained bull steep rally, with the 10y–7y spread returning to positive territory and widening to multi-year highs (in the order of ~25–30 bp depending on your series). This means that the 7-year yield has been falling faster than the 10-year yield, indicating a typical sign of normalization after a prolonged inversion, consistent with expectations of cuts and a market that is no longer putting pressure on the middle of the curve.

This metric is relevant to this fund because it maintains an effective duration of around 8–9 years, meaning that its ‘pain/benefit point’ lies precisely in the 7–10-year range. In a bull steepener regime such as the current one, both maturities tend to appreciate, but the 7-year maturity does so somewhat more; this favors the ‘belly’ of the curve and improves the carry. For an ‘enhanced AGG’ such as AGGH, which seeks the profile of the aggregate index and adds a tactical layer with futures/options, this context usually provides extra yield. If the regime were to shift to a bear steep (upward slope but with rising rates), the 7–10 segment would suffer in terms of price, and the ETF would feel the impact; that is why this metric is useful as a cycle thermometer to gauge whether the fund is sailing with the tide or against it.

The second metric we analyze is the adjustment of the yield curve, using the Nelson-Siegel-Svensson methodology. This adjustment gives us a clear picture of the curve, as it eliminates point-to-point noise and reveals its slope and curvature. The current shape is very telling. With short-term rates still high, around four and a half percent, there is a clear ‘valley’ in the 2-3 year range, where yields fall to around 3.7-3.8 percent. From there, we have a positive and fairly regular slope that leads to 7-10 years toward 4.1-4.3%, ending with a long term that stabilizes above 4.8-4.9%.

The current shape of the curve tells us that the market expects some relief for the economy and interest rate cuts in 1–2 years, which is why medium-term bonds (2–7 years) are yielding slightly less and there is a dip in the middle. In contrast, long maturities (15–30 years) continue to demand higher yields because there are doubts about the long term. This is consistent with normalization after the inverted curve phase. We are currently in the logical order of the curve – the longer the term, the higher the IRR – and that is good for bond investors because, in addition to the coupon, they recover two classic benefits: carry and roll-down, whereby the bond is worth more as it ‘ages’ if the curve remains steep.

For the vehicle analyzed, this geometry is key because its sensitivity lies precisely where the curve begins to slope, at 7-10 years. With an effective duration of around 8–9, the fund benefits from two fronts when the profile is as it is currently. The first is the attractive carry (the coupon versus a 10-year above 4%) and positive roll-down, a quiet gain obtained when a bond ‘rolls’ down an upward slope toward shorter maturities with lower IRR.

In addition, the team can fine-tune the duration with Treasury futures to anchor itself in that ‘sweet spot’ and, if implied volatility accompanies it, add the premium from its options overlay without betraying the core IG role. The risk lies in a shift to a bear steepener if the long end rebounds and the 10–30 segment moves upwards, or in a sudden drop in rates that compresses the slope too much and reduces option income. It also lies in the response of MBS and IG corporates, which tend to appreciate orderly curves but suffer if the market demands more yield for duration or credit.

Conclusion

In conclusion and to end this article, my recommendation is to hold on to the ETF (and, if you do not have it, start building a position gradually). The two metrics analyzed point to a more benign situation for an active core bond such as the one analyzed, since, as we have mentioned, the logical order of the curve is returning, carry, and roll-down are reappearing, and with a duration profile of 7–10 years, the fund is well positioned to capture coupon and some appreciation if the cuts come without any surprises.

The main nuance is that the term premium remains alive in the long term, and a spike in IRR due to fiscal or inflation noise could cause price bumps. With its options overlay, it helps with income but does not eliminate duration risk. Therefore, I would maintain the position with a 12–24-month horizon, add in tranches, and avoid excessive overweighting against liquidity or short bonds to cushion a possible pullback in the 20–30-year tranche. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Deutsche Post: The Upside Is Still Over 16% Per Year
data.description:
Sometimes you invest in a company that has incredible quality and history, but that company fails to return market-beating rates of profit despite buying it at what you view being a "solid" or even cheap price. Such was the case with my stake in Deutsche Post (OTCPK:DHLGY). This company has a position in the world logistics market that is matched only by UPS (UPS) and FedEx (FDX), one of these other companies I also own. In my last article, which is now almost exactly a year old, I submitted to you a performance of 30% in 2 years. This was "okay", 15% per year inclusive of dividends, nothing to "write home about". Since that year, the company has once again developed "okay" in that it was 15% or above, but not market-beating, because the SPY is up more than that.

If you view my investment over a period of over 4 years, you can see a very good rate of return in total, even if it's not entirely beating most indices. I would argue it's decent due to the safety of Deutsche Post/DHL, but the fact is that the company has "more room to move up". Even if risks have certainly ratcheted up - tariffs are one thing I will be focusing on in this piece, I will show you why, despite no longer giving 17-20% per year, I fully expect a market-beating rate of return to be possible for the company here.

Let's put this valuation and the results in the context of the current macro and see what we have here.

Deutsche Post DHL - Still an upside of 16%+ annually, with a yield of over 4.5%

I'm a fairly simple investor when it comes down to it. I like safe yields, safe returns, and safe upsides. Over 80% of my portfolio remains invested in companies that are viewed by the wider market and many of my analyst colleagues as very "boring", and that might even take several years to recover, but where I am simply happy collecting my dividends and waiting for that eventual outperformance.

Why?

Because usually it comes. KION AG (OTCPK:KIGRY) is the latest example of this, and that company hasn't even seen a full recovery to my fair value target yet, despite seeing a triple-digit rate of return from its lowest levels.

So it should be no surprise that when one of the largest logistics companies on the planet remains undervalued, I remain positive for it. Arguments against this stance, those that would view DHL as being risky and not worthy of your capital as an investment, would argue that the company's growth is "over", and the company has revision risks (from things like the tariffs). They would also argue that the company has a risky, or high payout ratio for its dividend, about the current protectionism and isolationism, which is likely to impact global trade trends, as well as coming job cuts and efficiency targets, implying operational issues.

Countering this, bulls like me would argue for the company's recent results prior to 2Q, profitability metrics that are among the best in the industry, and the decent (4.5%+) and well-covered yield. Furthermore, I would argue that the bearish assumption of growth challenges is founded in too much negativity - I instead expect top-line growth thanks to Life Science and E-commerce trends, which remain solid arguments for growth even at this time.

But the latest results, 2Q25, really speak for themselves.

Top-line growth seems consistent and ensured here. The company's 2Q25 results also include, as seen above, a 6% YoY EBIT growth, and the 5% or above EBIT growth for 1H25 is consistent as well.

But the volatility on the market is visible in other ways. The volume development is very much up and down, with some sub-segments down 20% while others, like EC B2C Europe, are up 11%. The e-commerce trend in the consumer segment, which is responsible for growth and profit in consumer segments, is intact, even if growing more slowly than before. The tariffs and enacted hindrances from both China, Hongkong and the US are coming in at only a very limited overall impact.

At the same time that the company is growing top-line and bottom-line results, the efficiency program, or cost program, is moving along according to plan. Structural cost improvement and personnel savings thanks to digitization, AI and restructuring is ongoing, and the company recently confirmed its overall run rate savings of €1B at the end of 2026. The guidance for 2025 is unchanged, and at this point, it's something we can see as "confirmed" in my view, as I believe the likelihood for further structural volatility is low.

For a few years, the company's focus has also been on Free cash flow - and this focus has been paying off in spades. The company's conversion history has improved from lows of 7% to over 85-90%.

So I have a hard time seeing, beyond macro risks, what would cause the company to cyclically down-trend in terms of EPS, which is, by the way, also what current estimates are forecasting (which we'll go into later). To any argument that it might get worse - of course it could - but as of 2Q25, the company is already seeing that lower volume impact, and has managed it successfully with a 5%+ EBIT growth, entirely offsetting the weaker US trade. If this trend continues, then it's conceivable that the trend will only weaken DHL slightly as opposed to what some seem to believe.

This goes into my general belief that Europe, if the current geopolitical trend moves forward, will have an easier time "decoupling" from the US as a trade partner than vice versa.

Why?

Because the EU is less reliant on the US than the US is on the EU. The US relies heavily on European goods and services, as confirmed by current trade deficits. The EU also has a far greater resilience to any tariffs compared to the US reliance, a broader economic region with better strategic positioning for alternatives, a current political move to focus on autonomy, and a very good potential to build alternative alliances, which are already ongoing.

Anyone who believes that the EU is heavily reliant on the US in any way that precludes the Union from decoupling simply needs to look at a world map, the data, and trading data, as well as what is ongoing. This is key to DHL as well, because even if logistic flows to the US would be further impaired and this would appear in the company valuation, I would view this as an investment opportunity.

Of course, a decoupling would result in significant potential investment opportunities in the US as well. This is not a political consideration for me, but instead an opportunistic one, as I always am.

I believe 2Q25 confirms the positive thesis for DHL, and the company's performance further has cemented a potential upside, as follows.

Let's therefore look at valuation.

Valuation for DHL - Upside remains

DHL tends to trade and follow a 13-16x P/E. Based on the credit rating, an over 4.5% well-covered yield, and the upside from growth currently estimated at over 5-7% per year until 2027E from savings and efficiencies, I consider this range as valid, with a target of around 15x, which is also the 10-12 year P/E average for that business.

Based on a 2027E forecast, this 15x P/E comes to a fair value of about €55/share. To get a 15% annualized upside or above from this, we need to invest at a €43/share or below - and at €39/share we have a good margin of error for that PT, even if it's higher than when I previously bought shares.

Forecasting to my PT, the annualized rate of return is potentially at 18-19% per year.

And this is obviously, based on my requirements, a good enough rate of return for me to continue investing. The downside here is limited. DHL would have to go significantly lower, or not be able to grow its EPS, for returns to go below double digits here. Because the company's upside to EPS is primarily internal as opposed to dependent on volume growth or specific macro trends, I view these growth estimates as likely.

Alternatives in logistics would come to UPS and FedEx, both mentioned earlier. I'm invested in UPS, which is a reversal investment. I also consider this a "BUY", but I view DHL as safer, despite the impressive yield of over 4% and potential higher rate of return to a similar P/E.

To summarize, this company remains, to me, attractive even in the context of what else is available. DHL is lower risk than UPS (though I own both). I reiterate my DHL fair value of €55/share, and a PT of €43/share, which means the company is currently a "BUY". For the DHLGY ADR, this comes to a PT of $50/share.

Risks to the thesis

Any thesis and "Buy" have risks, and DHL is no different. Because of DHL's large exposure to packages and the like, I would view the primary risk to a positive thesis as a continued low level of package volume. Due in part to increasing geopolitical tensions even in 2025-2026, it's hard to say exactly at what level this will normalize. The EU and several EU nations have introduced punitive fees and tolls on import packages, which is pressuring volumes here. B2B is seeing similar muted trends.

Some tech-savvy investors would also calculate the risk of Amazon as an entrant as a logistics player in Europe as something to consider. As things stand now, it can be mentioned, but I don't believe quick moves here are likely.

But taking all this in, I say that the positives outweigh the negatives, and the following DHL thesis is valid as of 2Q25.

Thesis

  • Even just considering a 14-15X forward upside, that RoR is still potentially over 15% - and that's with the yield at 4.5%. As always, I believe the native through IBKR or one of the brokers that accept EU stocks is the way to go here.
  • Investing in Deutsche Post DHL means exposure to the attractive EU logistics and legacy post/parcel market. This is not going anywhere, despite the current issues. The yield is well-covered, and the company has already reported excellent run-rate 2Q25 results. It also continues to deliver solid guidance for the rest of the year, but above everything, for the next 2-3 years.
  • At below 15X P/E, you're locking in the capital at a very low multiple, with a 15%+ annualized upside - and that's what I'm all about. The PT for DHLGY is $50/share.

Remember, I'm all about:

  1. Buying undervalued - even if that undervaluation is slight and not mind-numbingly massive - companies at a discount, allowing them to normalize over time and harvesting capital gains and dividends in the meantime.

  2. If the company goes well beyond normalization and goes into overvaluation, I harvest gains and rotate my position into other undervalued stocks, repeating #1.

  3. If the company doesn't go into overvaluation but hovers within a fair value, or goes back down to undervaluation, I buy more as time allows.

  4. I reinvest proceeds from dividends, savings from work, or other cash inflows as specified in #1.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.

  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.

  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.

  • This company is currently cheap.

  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of €43/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of August 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: LENZ Therapeutics: Adjusting My Position After Almost Doubling In Four Months
data.description:
Article Thesis

I wrote about LENZ Therapeutics (NASDAQ:LENZ) at the beginning of April, with the stock at ~$21. The company was in a very advanced phase of approval with their aceclidine-based eye drop LNZ100 for presbyopia, the loss of near vision that comes with aging.

My base-case Fair Value estimate derived from a 10-year DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) model was $42.55, based entirely on LNZ100 estimates, and incorporating an 82% probability factor, since the drug was not approved at that time. With ~101% potential upside to Fair Value, I initiated a stock position, while maintaining the position size at ~0.25x of a normal position, for what I call “Extreme Uncertainty”: a company with a single product, and not even in a commercial phase.

Two things happened in the meantime: FDA approved its product, and the stock nearly doubled. What is the updated Fair Value after this event? What is the new position size that I desire? Can we add some hedging or another options position?

Business Highlights

I described the benefits of LNZ100 in my initial article, so I won’t insist on that again. In the meantime, not only that FDA approved their product, but they also executed multiple international license and commercialization agreements:

  • Agreement with Lotus Pharmaceuticals for commercialization in Korea and parts of South-East Asia for $195M in upfront and milestone payments, as well as royalties.
  • Agreement with Laboratoires Théa for commercialization in Canada, for $70M in upfront and milestone payments, as well as royalties.
  • Submission of NDA for LNZ100 in China through their partnership with CORXEL Pharmaceuticals, for up to $95M of regulatory and sales milestones, as well as royalties.

These add to a potential US total addressable market of 128M presbyopes:

With interesting perspectives in both US and international markets, it’s normal that LENZ is focusing now on their strategy for commercialization. In fact, they were already actively preparing their sales force even before the approval, as mentioned in their last press release:

The Company has completed the hiring and pre-approval training of the 88-member sales force and is prepared to initiate sales and marketing activities immediately upon approval in the United States.

Financial Highlights

The company is estimating at least $3B market size based on conservative 6% US adoption:

On one side, I agree that 6% is at least a conservative assumption, since their product is better than the current treatment based on pilocarpine (Vuity), which has the side effect of inducing myopia. And we didn’t even incorporate international markets.

On the other side, I think the current Vuity pricing ($79/month) could be a difficult target to achieve, since we could see more competitors, which I mentioned in my initial article. I think that the two sides neutralize each other, so I’m comfortable with a conservative $3B.

Like mentioned in detail in my initial article, I assume that LENZ will capture ~30% of this market, with the rest spread among current and future competitors. And without any probability factor anymore after the FDA approval, that gives us ~$900M potential peak sales.

I find a little odd that there are near to zero long-term upward revisions after the FDA approval:

First, there are only 3 analysts for 2032-2034. Now, maybe they were close to 100% sure that the product would be approved and they didn’t include a probability factor like I did. Or maybe they think the cycle until peak sales will be longer than 10 years (that’s possible, with a gradually aging population), so those $691M from 2034 could further expand until patent expiration in 2039.

On the profitability front, after one-two years with lower scale and sampling, I assume a healthy 77% gross margin, in-line with 77%-80% for other small biotech companies.

With all this advertising and education activity, it’s normal that Sales & Marketing expenses will be very high for a few years, so I expect them to become profitable in 2028. Then, I assume S&M gradually increasing in absolute dollars but gradually decreasing as a margin until 23% in 2034, very slightly higher than 20% than I modeled initially, based on slightly higher expected inflation.

Looking at their current cash position of $209.5M, combined with no debt, I think that could be sufficient until cash-flow profitability, so I expect only minor dilution for funding operations.

Fair Value

In my base-case model, I use a 10-year DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) model with the following assumptions:

  • Revenue: Peak sales of $900M in 2034 (see above section).
  • Gross margin: Constantly growing to a peak of 77% in 2028 and beyond (see above section).
  • Operating margin: profitability reached in 2028 and a terminal EBIT margin of ~51%, based on declining S&M (see above section).
  • Tax rate: 24%.
  • CapEx: $300M spread until 2027 for expanding operations.
  • Growth in perpetuity: -2.5%, due to patent protection loss in 2039.
  • Discount rate (100% Cost of Equity): 12.5%, in line with other small biotech companies with only one product.

The resulting Fair Value is $52.

Alternatively, I use $738M sales in 2034, like in my initial article before the FDA approval and closer to consensus, followed by 0% growth in perpetuity, based on the above premise that growth will continue after 2034 (until patent expiration) due to a gradually aging population. In this model, Fair Value is $48.1.

By averaging the two, we have a base-case Fair Value of ~$50.

For a slightly optimistic scenario, I model the following changes:

  • Revenues from international operations: the amounts in the above section from their partnerships for Asia, Canada, and China (more to come?) spread through 6 years, followed by royalties of $50M by year.
  • Growth in perpetuity 0% (instead of -2.5%).
  • Tax rate: 21% (instead of 24%).

With the other assumptions unchanged, Fair Value estimate would be $66.7

For a slightly pessimistic scenario, I model only 20% market share from that conservative $3B market seen by the company. Keeping -2.5% as growth in perpetuity, we have a Fair Value estimate of $36.

Strategy and Risks

As mentioned in my initial article, I initiated a 0.25x position at the beginning of April. With the stock at ~$21, I saw it as a “Buy”, but their product was not approved at that time. The stock nearly doubled in about four months, it was near $42 twice last month, and it is at ~$39 today.

My position naturally grew close to 0.5x and I mentioned in my initial article that I would target a position size of 0.5x after the FDA approval. However, that was for the “Buy” area, and I see LENZ in the Hold area between $36 (slightly pessimistic Fair Value) and $66 (slightly optimistic Fair Value). Considering the risk of a single product company (even if approved, if something bad happens with that product, that would be catastrophic for the company), I trimmed about one third of my position yesterday at $39.5, for about 88% return in a bit more than four months.

My next movements could be:

  • If the stock moves back in the “Buy” zone, I could increase again my position to ~0.5x, but not immediately under $36, perhaps around $30.
  • I could increase my exposure even more than 0.5x if the stock drops under $20, although I think this is a very unlikely scenario (without major fundamental changes).
  • I sometimes sell covered Call options on these high growth stocks, because I can collect high premiums due to high IV (Implied Volatility), but only after they rally enough from my undervalued “Buy” moment until around base-case Fair Value. For example, for LENZ I could sell a Call option with the stock around $50. Or maybe, with the stock above $45, a slightly OTM (Out-of-The-Money) 50 Call option to give it a little more room to grow and yet to still collect an interesting premium.
  • I would sell my entire position above the slightly optimistic Fair Value, which is $66 today (if I didn’t sell a 50 Call option).

Takeaway

LENZ Therapeutics received FDA approval for their eye drop LNZ100 for presbyopia, lowering the risk of the investment. Besides, they have some international commercialization agreements and they are aggressively building their sales team.

With a 6% penetration in the U.S. addressable market and 30% market share for LENZ, they could have peak sales modeled of ~$900M by 2034. LENZ's solid cash position and conservative market share assumptions limit dilution risk, and I expect profitability by 2028.

My new base-case Fair Value estimate, derived from a 10-year DCF model with -2.5% growth in perpetuity, is $50. In a slightly optimistic scenario, where we incorporate revenues from the international operations and 0% growth in perpetuity, Fair Value would be $66. In a slightly pessimistic scenario, with only 20% US market share, Fair Value would be $36.

After a near-doubling in share price in a bit more than four months, my position grew close to 0.5x, and I trimmed one third at $39.5. I rate LENZ as a Hold between $36 and $66, and I might consider selling a covered Call when the stock approaches its base-case Fair Value of $50.

data.title: Broadstone Net Lease: Repricing Means Rerating (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Several weeks ago, I published an article on Broadstone Net Lease (NYSE:BNL) where I said that the company was thriving from the doldrums. The purpose of the article was to explore a REIT that I believed was trading at a steep discount to peers. In fact, I pointed out that BNL was trading at one of the cheapest adjusted funds from operations, or AFFO, multiples in the sector despite doing a lot of things right.

This article will follow up on prior coverage and reassess the valuation after a significant repricing.

Review of Prior Coverage

My prior coverage of BNL was published on August 8th and is requisite reading for today’s discussion. Over the past several weeks, BNL’s stock has broken out while the remainder of the net lease sector treads water amid volatile interest rates. Let’s take a step back and see where things stood shortly ago.

I thought BNL was sitting in the net lease penalty box as Mr. Market was pricing BNL at around $16 per share throughout July and into August. Despite the decline in share price over the preceding 12 months, there were two key reasons that I felt BNL was positioned to do well in the near term and long term.

First and foremost, I thought management was being creative. This meant reassessing the portfolio with a willingness to get their hands dirty. BNL’s management was discussing potential portfolio sales and new development opportunities where the company could effectively recycle capital. After the company’s successful transformation into a retail/industrial net lease REIT over the past several years, I think management had done a good job of effectively managing their portfolio.

Second, I thought the company was trading at a steep discount to peers. In the past, I have discussed why I think BNL’s portfolio is weaker than key competitors. The assets and tenants are generally weaker from a credit and real estate perspective. While I thought the company deserved to be priced at a discount to REITs like Agree Realty (ADC) or STAG Industrial (STAG), an adjusted funds from operations per share multiple of 10x seemed punishingly low. For investors this translated to a dividend yield of around 7%.

My conclusion from several weeks ago can be found below.

I think BNL deserves more credit than they have received. The company’s management appears to recognize the capital issues facing the company and are actively pivoting towards other funding sources that could allow them to keep their pipeline open. Rather than continue blindly or close up shop, BNL is adapting to a difficult environment.

In the meantime, investors who are willing to accept the risk of investing in a brand that is a work in progress have an opportunity for significant multiple expansion. Currently, BNL is trading at a discount to the sector median providing ample upside should Mr. Market decide to change his tune on BNL.

REITs are an industry which go through periods of stability followed by periods of volatility. BNL is no different as the company has illustrated over the past several weeks.

The Market Changes Its Mind

Over the past several years, BNL found itself in the penalty box. As the company embarked on a strategic plan to ditch their medical office portfolio in large tranches and smaller transactions, earnings became less certain and the board elected to freeze the dividend. Given net lease investors flock for the growing income, the company was assigned a discounted multiple while REITs like ADC and Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) continues raising their payouts while maintaining more conservative payout ratios.

This and a combination of other factors disappointed the market and over the past five years, shares of the company remained at a virtual stand still.

Oh, how quickly things can change. Over the past month, shares of BNL have appreciated by nearly 15% as the market begins to assess the risk profile of one of the market’s newer net lease REITs. Despite a bumpy several years for BNL, the company appears to be emerging stronger and gaining recognition from the market.

For those lucky enough to have purchased shares of BNL during the summer months, we have materially outperformed a struggling sector.

Despite positive news around near term interest rate cuts, the net lease sector in general remains in the penalty box. A few days ago, I published an article on the real estate sector more broadly, discussing how stabilizing capitalization rates in net lease could be beneficial for commercial real estate more broadly. As other real estate sectors break out, net lease remains stagnant given the movement of short term rates will not necessarily move the long term rates that typically price net lease.

After a difficult year, it appears as though BNL was begun to climb out of its hole. But where do things stand today and does the company’s pricing still offer value.

Reassessing After Repricing

Over the past month, BNL shares have appreciated by nearly 15%, marking a significant rally in a steady sector. It is unusual for the share price of a net lease REIT to move this significantly during a short period of time. When it does happen, it generally demands a reassessment.

As of my prior publication, BNL was trading at a share price of just under $17 corresponding to an adjusted funds from operations per share multiple of between 10x and 11x. The forward multiple based on projected growth was 11x. Compared to peers in the net lease sector like STAG or ADC which were trading at 16x and 18x AFFO, BNL looked cheap. Even compared against other peers like W.P. Carey (WPC) and Realty Income (O), which both traded at 13.3x AFFO, BNL looked like a steal.

The past month has repriced net lease and today, the value matrix looks much different. Below is an updated chart of other net lease REITs and their current per share metrics.

Today, BNL’s new valuation puts the company roughly in line with other participants in net lease. For example, NNN REIT (NNN) is trading at the same AFFO per share multiple as BNL. O and WPC trade slightly higher in the 13x AFFO per share range. Compared to just one month ago when BNL was trading several times lower each of these peers, the valuation picture looks materially different today.

Reminding ourselves that BNL has the highest dividend payout ratio of the REITs listed in the chart above, the company still faces issues that could affect future growth. While management has already outlined their plans to navigate these challenges including balance sheet management and capital recycling, the company now lacks a valuation catalyst compared to other peers. Personally, I find NNN more attractive at the same AFFO per share multiple and a payout ratio of just 70%. While I think ADC and EPRT remain expensive, O, the largest net lease REIT, still trades around 13x AFFO per share, a significant discount compared to the company’s historical valuation.

While I think opportunity exists across net lease, the recent repricing of the sector has rearranged the risk reward hierarchy, especially for BNL. While BNL remains a good opportunity in a diverse sector, the company now lacks a compelling catalyst compared to other high quality peers. I would prefer to allocate capital towards NNN or O currently. BNL earns a Hold rating.

data.title: Archer Aviation: A Calculated Risk Worth Taking
data.description:
Archer Aviation (NYSE:ACHR) is what I believe to be the most compelling contrarian opportunity in the eVTOL space. While the market is focused on Joby Aviation's (JOBY) flight hours and earlier certification timeline, Archer's Q2 results showed us something far more important, which is tangible execution across multiple fronts that the market is undervaluing.

At a $6 billion market cap, Archer trades at a meaningful discount to Joby's premium valuation, yet it's executing on what matters most, the actual commercialization. On paper, Joby looks like the clear winner, which is exactly why the market has awarded them a premium valuation while leaving Archer trading at a meaningful discount at just a $6 billion market cap.

What really matters in this industry isn't who has the most impressive specs or the most flight hours logged. What matters is who can actually commercialize this technology and generate revenue. And on that front, Archer's has something far more important than any flight statistics, in my opinion, since they're executing on actual commercialization while everyone else is still essentially running science experiments.

They have six Midnight aircraft in production, three in final assembly, the first aircraft delivered to Abu Dhabi and selection as the Official Air Taxi Provider for the LA28 Olympics, they aren't just building prototypes anymore. This gives them both a deadline and a global showcase for their technology. Right now they are burning cash but they are backed by $1.72 billion in liquidity after their recent $850 million raise, they have more cash than any competitor in the space.

Why This Phase Is Actually The Perfect Entry Point

The stock has been stuck in what I call an "identity crisis" up 133% over the past year for early investors, but essentially flat YTD. The market is treating Archer like it's still a speculative research project, but they have already crossed into the execution phase.

Let me walk you through the numbers that matter. Yes, they lost $206 million in Q2 with an adjusted EBITDA loss of $118.7 million. The adjusted EBITDA loss of $118.7 million widened from $93.8 million in the prior year, which looks like accelerating cash burn.

But First let me break down what's actually happening with that $206 million loss. About $92 million of that loss was from non-cash charges with $82 million specifically from warrant liability revaluations which means that actual cash wasn’t leaving the company.

Their net loss excluding these non-cash items was about $114 million. The actual cash used in operating and investing activities was $127 million for the quarter.

When you consider they are building an entirely new industry from scratch, running manufacturing facilities in both Georgia and California, conducting international flight tests, and working through one of the most complex certification processes in aviation history, it is an impressively disciplined capital allocation.

Morgan Stanley projected that the eVTOL market could reach $9 trillion by 2050. Now, I'm usually skeptical of such projections because we have all heard the "flying cars”, personal jetpacks for everyone by 2020 promises before.

But this time, there are three main differences that make me believe we are at a genuine inflection point. First, the technology actually works because Archer has proven with hundreds of test flights that electric vertical takeoff and landing is not just possible but practical.

Second, the regulatory framework is materializing with both the FAA and international regulators like the GCAA actively engaged in certification processes. Third, and most importantly, the money is already flowing from serious institutional players who don't make billion-dollar bets on science fiction. United Airlines committed to buy up to 200 aircraft, Stellantis invested hundreds of millions and provided manufacturing expertise. That's why I think it is not speculation anymore, due to the validation from sophisticated investors who seem to have done their due diligence and seem to have concluded this market is real.

Near-Term Revenue That Will Change Everything

While everyone obsesses over FAA certification timelines, Archer just pulled off something brilliant with their Launch Edition program. They're not waiting for U.S. certification to generate revenue.

On the Q2 earnings call, CEO Adam Goldstein said that.

“this summer in the UAE, and we now have signed definitive revenue-generating agreements with both Abu Dhabi Aviation and Audio. And that sets us up for low tens of millions of dollars in payments over the next 18 to 24 months, with a portion of that starting this year …we've delivered our first Midnight to UAE in June, and you can expect us to ramp up a small fleet in Abu Dhabi going forward.”

It would change them from a research project into an operating business.

What's particularly clever about starting in the UAE is the operational environment itself provides valuable data for global deployment.

During their July test flights at Al Bateen Airport in Abu Dhabi, internal components within Midnight's avionics bay heated to over 140 degrees Fahrenheit immediately before takeoff.

Testing in these extreme conditions like high temperature, high humidity, dust exposure provides validation for their technology. If Midnight can operate reliably in the UAE summer it can operate anywhere.

This strategy is how it de-risks the entire operation. By deploying aircraft commercially under GCAA authorization before FAA certification Archer gets real world operational data, customer feedback and most importantly, revenue. I believe it's the difference between a science project and a business.

For a pre-revenue company, this near term monetization completely changes the investment narrative. That’s why I think it is the best time to invest in Archer because by the time they start making money, it will be too late because the market would have already priced this in.

The Defense Opportunity Nobody's Pricing In

Here's where things get really interesting, and where I think the market is completely asleep. Over the last 18 months Archer has quietly built what could become a massive defense business. The Pentagon has requested $13.4 billion for autonomous military systems with the majority allocated to aircraft.

Archer's recent strategic acquisitions suddenly make perfect sense in this context. The Overair patent portfolio and technical team they acquired came from Karem Aircraft, founded by Abe Karem who invented the Predator drone and is considered the founding father of drone technology with decades of DARPA and DoD relationships.

CEO Adam Goldstein said this on Q2 call:

"I can't say much about it, but We see this as a global opportunity, not just an opportunity in the U.S... we think it represents a very exciting opportunity. …the opportunity to be very large and very large in the early parts of the Archer revenue generating process."

Read between the lines here. While they can't say much due to the nature of defense contracts, their strategic acquisitions and Pentagon's budget allocations hint enough that something big is happening in my view.

The Discount To Joby Makes No Sense

Joby maintains a premium valuation despite Archer's superior execution momentum. Yes, Joby has logged 40,000 miles and completed 21 piloted full transition flights in Dubai. Their S4 aircraft has a 150 mile range versus Midnight's 60 miles. But none of that matters if you can't commercialize effectively, in my view.

Archer's short timeline is actually an advantage. They started later but learned from others' mistakes. While Joby spent years perfecting specs, Archer is focused on what airlines actually need. They don’t need 150 mile range aircraft for 10 mile urban routes, but a practical, certifiable design that can be manufactured at scale.

Joby entered the certification process earlier, but both companies are now in what the FAA calls the "final stages of policy development" and there are only two companies at this stage.

Archer has already achieved about 15% completion of their compliance verification documents and successfully completed multiple SOI-3 audits, including for their in-house developed powertrain software and hardware. These SOI-3 audits are the basically last step before final certification review. The gap between Joby and Archer in terms of actual certification progress is months.

But commercially, Archer's Launch Edition program will be generating revenue before certification while Joby waits for FAA approval. At current price, I believe you are essentially getting Archer's UAE revenue stream for free compared to Joby's valuation. This misalignment won't last for long in my opinion.

What Could Go Wrong?

I usually don’t invest in pre-revenue companies working on experimental projects unless I have a very high conviction and a deeper understanding of the tech itself. In the case of Archer they have moved out of the prototyping phase and are preparing to enter the market. Now investments like these carry huge execution risks. I am okay with taking that risk but if you don’t understand the tech, the market and what they are doing then you should not invest.

I'm bullish, but I'm not blind to the risks. And at current burn rates of around $130 million quarterly, that $1.7 billion gives them roughly three years of runway. But here's why I'm not worried. UAE revenues start this year, and defense contracts could materialize within the next two years.

Every quarter of delay would cost them millions. But Archer's Launch Edition strategy provides a buffer. They can generate revenue internationally while awaiting FAA approval. Plus, with only two companies (Archer and Joby) in the final certification stages, the FAA has an incentive to move quickly to validate the industry.

Now, the biggest risk is: will people actually fly in these things? The UAE deployment answers this definitively yes. If sovereign wealth funds are betting billions on consumer adoption, I'll take that validation.

data.title: Perpetua Resources: Idaho Facilities Nearing Development, Supported By Vibrant Gold And Antimony
data.description:
A "Buy" Rating for Perpetua Resources Corp.

With this article, we are upgrading the previously assigned “Hold” rating for Perpetua Resources Corp. (NASDAQ:PPTA), a stock listed on the NASDAQ under the symbol “PPTA” and now believe that a “Buy” rating for this stock is most appropriate, provided potential investors continue to strengthen their positions on the dips.

In its report “ Second Quarter 2025 and Recent Highlights”, Perpetua Resources described itself as follows:

Perpetua Resources Corp., through its wholly owned subsidiaries, is focused on the exploration, site restoration and redevelopment of gold-antimony-silver deposits in the Stibnite-Yellow Pine district of central Idaho that are encompassed by the Stibnite Gold Project. The Stibnite Gold Project is one of the highest-grade, open pit gold deposits in the United States and is designed to apply a modern, responsible mining approach to restore an abandoned mine site and produce both gold and the only mined source of antimony in the United States. Antimony trisulfide from Stibnite is the only known domestic reserves of antimony that can meet U.S. defense needs for many small arms, munitions, and missile types.

Perpetua Resources is headquartered in Boise, Idaho, USA.

How The Stock Has Developed Since Our Last Article: The Upward Catalysts Have Worked In Favour As Expected

Since our last article on February 9, 2025, shares of Nasdaq-listed PPTA Perpetua Resources Corp. have experienced a rapid rise, gaining 49.76% and outperforming the S&P 500’s +6.94% at the time of this writing. This is due to the positive factors we highlighted: the upward momentum in gold and antimony, as well as relevant progress in the development of the Stibnite gold project for future production of gold and antimony, critical to Western investment hedging strategies and U.S. defense needs. Several factors have further strengthened demand for gold as a safe haven, including geopolitical uncertainty surrounding the two major conflict outbreaks in Ukraine and the Middle East, as well as the threat of tariffs, and central banks' continued robust gold purchases. The gold price per ounce has reflected this. The benchmark for gold's performance is Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR), which has risen by about 18% since our last article on February 9, 2025, and was at $3,380.87/ounce at the time of writing.

Interest in antimony trisulfide (“stibnite”) has recently improved due to its important role in various applications, such as alloys for batteries, plastics, solar panels, semiconductors, flame retardants, pigments, and glass. However, the growing appeal of this raw material is certainly due to its usage in defence applications. The supply of antimony, on the other hand, is limited by supply chain disruptions, particularly since China, which accounts for a large share of global mining and processing thanks to its large reserves, imposed export restrictions on this mineral last year. Strong demand for this raw material, combined with limited supply, is likely to drive up its price, which varies considerably depending on quantity, purity, and supplier. A pound of this raw material could cost $55 or approximately $150 per kg, with very high purity reaching $555.77/kg. But by purchasing in bulk, this price could be lower, and a metric ton could cost around $20,000.

In our previous analysis, we also recommended buying on dips, which are always possible given the volatility experienced by Perpetua Resources’ stock price on the NASDAQ stock exchange, just like the gold commodity market. This allows the investor to benefit more from rising gold prices when buying shares that are not in a sharp downturn. Since February 9, 2025, the date of our previous review, Perpetua's share price has reached significantly lower levels than at that time, on several occasions. Had the investor added his holdings of Perpetua Resources' NASDAQ-listed common shares and profited from these price declines, his investment would likely have outperformed the U.S. equity market by more than the margin indicated above at the time of this writing.

The Rating Upgrade: Massive Capital Injection Through Equity Offering and Private Placement and Progress In The Permitting Phase

The rating upgrade is primarily due to the high liquidity recently acquired by Perpetua Resources. The company has raised $474 million for its mining project in Idaho through $425 million in gross proceeds from the equity financing ($325 million public offering of 24,622,000 common shares plus a $100 million private placement of 7,575,757 common shares to Paulson & Co. Inc.) and subsequently additional gross proceeds of $49 million through the full exercise of an underwriter's option. This $474 million is not the only financing Perpetua Resources Corp. is receiving as part of a comprehensive financing package for the development of its Stibnite gold project in Idaho. The company also applied for project financing of up to $2 billion from the Export-Import Bank of the United States (“EXIM”) in May 2025. Following the capital raising through equity offerings, EXIM's due diligence is expected to be successful, and PPTA will probably receive the green light for the debt financing in 2026.

The Stibnite Gold Project: Crucial For Investor Strongholds And The US In Its International Trade Relations

If approved, EXIM debt financing could fund a majority of the Project's development costs to become the only domestically mined source of antimony. (Perpetua Resources Corp. said on May 23, 2025, via PRNewswire and reported by Seeking Alpha.)

The project involves converting a brownfield property in Idaho, home to rich gold and stibnite deposits for the production of munitions and other light weapons, into a massive deposit of these two metals. The mine is said to have had over 100 years of mining activity (before being abandoned), a characteristic of the presence of rich mineral resources and in quantities that justify economic exploitation.

In a report on the final federal permit Perpetua Resources received for its Stibnite gold and antimony project, Seeking Alpha wrote the following about the Stibnite project's resources on May 20, 2025:

The Stibnite project is projected to be one of the highest-grade open-pit gold mines in the U.S., with 4.8M oz of gold reserves, and is expected to produce ~450K oz/year of gold over its first four years of production.

The project also holds an antimony reserve estimated at 148M lbs, the only identified antimony reserve in the U.S. and one of the largest reserves outside of Chinese control; the project could meet 35% of U.S. antimony demand during its initial six years of production.

Thanks to its vast resource base, Perpetua can produce gold and stibnite at its Stibnite Gold project, located 159 kilometres northeast of Boise, Idaho. The project already has significant risk mitigation factors, as it is located in an area with other companies and has good infrastructure for metal extraction and production. Idaho also represents a low risk for exploration and mining, similar to all other states in the United States.

The Stibnite Gold Project is strategically well positioned to meet US demand for gold, and especially stibnite. With the Perpetua Resources’ project in Idaho, the US now has the opportunity to start to effectively counter China. If the US were to produce stibnite itself, the North American economy would have large reserves to produce this raw material in relevant quantities and start to meet domestic industrial demand. The US has a strong interest in pursuing this goal, as its economy would then no longer be exposed to the risk of Chinese export restrictions.

In the deep and widespread uncertainty plaguing the global economy today, the two metals will share a common demand factor, reviving ancient, now extremely useful properties of both elements:

Gold will be used by investors as a safe haven, as its appreciation on the precious metals market allows them to protect the value of their investments from the adverse winds of the extremely volatile global environment. Assuming deglobalization, geopolitical conflicts and trade disputes justify the demand for safe assets, along with lower interest rates benefiting zero-yield bullion as the Federal Reserve joins other Western central banks in cutting rates in the future, the gold price should remain robust in the coming years or eventually rise faster. Recently, InvestingHaven-dot-com analysts have predicted gold price targets for the upcoming years: $3,500 in 2025, near $3,900 in 2026 and $5,155 in 2030. When Perpetua Resources' Stibnite Gold projects enter commercial production, the gold price, driven by positive factors, should be around $4,527.5/ounce or 34% above current levels, according to InvestingHaven-dot-com analysts. As the share price of Perpetua Resources or PPTA common stock is characterized by a positive correlation with the gold price during its development phase from the financing announcement (expected with EXIM debt financing in 2026) to the first gold pour (expected between 2028/2029), it will have the tailwind to keep on rising, in line with what happened with other gold stocks (see later screenshot showing Artemis, G-Mining and Skeena).

Stibnite will be used for the production of munitions and other military equipment. The US, in line with prevailing sentiment in other countries, in a global climate of great uncertainty, feels the need to erect barriers to protect its own economy, not only through protectionist trade policies but also by transforming its own economy into a war economy. This means higher government spending to strengthen domestic arms production capacity, in line with the trend we are observing: the US reached its historic peak of $997,309 million last year after a dramatic jump in military spending in less than a decade.

The Permitting Phase Is Largely Complete, And With Financing, The Road Is Solidly Paved For Construction Activities

The stibnite project is completing the permitting phase and could begin construction before the end of 2025. With the issuance of the final federal permit in May 2025 by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, this phase can also be considered nearly complete and successful. Final federal approval paves the way for a promising future for Perpetua Resources' stibnite project. Along with other US projects originally selected by the Trump administration and reviewed under the expedited process, Perpetua Resources' Stibnite project is seen as an opportunity to boost domestic production of critical minerals. In its June 16, 2025, announcement of a “[…] US$425 Million Financing as part of Comprehensive Financing Package for Stibnite Gold Project”, Perpetua Resources Corp. wrote:

Together with the EXIM debt financing and royalty financing described below, if successfully completed in the amounts anticipated, the Company believes that the net proceeds from the Offering and the Private Placement will provide the Company with sufficient capital to fund the Project construction costs of US$2.2 billion, along with additional funds for a cost-over run account, debt service, working capital costs in excess of the Project capital costs and exploration activities.

In addition to the US$155 million sought by Perpetua in guarantee and indemnification as required by regulators, from which the company is awaiting a few other government approvals, Perpetua wrote that it expects to raise:

proceeds between US$200 million to US$250 million, in exchange for either a gold net smelter return ("NSR") royalty not to exceed 3.9% or a gold stream. (Source: “Perpetua Resources Closes US$425 Million Financing as part of Comprehensive Financing Package for Stibnite Gold Project.” Perpetua Resources Corp., June 16, 2025, via CNW, Seeking Alpha reports.)

Stibnite Gold: First Gold Pour In 2028/2029, But With Further Value To Unlock Through Development

Perpetua Resources is expected to begin construction of the Stibnite Gold and Antimony production facility in Idaho by the end of 2025, and the project will reach commercial production between 2028 and 2029. The robust outlook for gold prices in particular and the critical use of antimony in various industrial applications, including the production of munitions during the mobilization to a war economy, will help ensure that the market valuation of this stock remains robust during the development phase. The company demonstrates in the screenshot below how other projects have had the same fate: Between the financing announcement and the subsequent first gold pour, Artemis' share price rose by 125.7% and G Mining's by 252.8% within a few years. Skeena Resources Limited's share price, which is $15.89 per share at the time of writing, has not yet reached commercial production, but has risen by 171.6% since the financing announcement on June 25, 2024.

What The Stibnite Gold Project Could Look Like Operationally And Financially

The Stibnite Gold project will produce 4,223 koz of gold over the 15-year mine life or 296 koz of gold/year or 463 koz/year in the first 4 years of production, while antimony production will account for 107 million pounds over the 15-year mine life. In its first four years of production, Perpetua Resources' Stibnite Gold Project will play a significant role in the global gold mining landscape, comparing its profile as a future mining operation with the following operators, assuming they deliver on their promises: A) Lundin Gold's Fruta del Norte mine in Ecuador has an annual production forecast of 475,000–525,000 ounces and AISC guidance of $935 to $995 per oz sold for the 2025 year. B) Centamin's Sukari gold mine in Egypt targets average annual production of 506,000 ounces at an AISC of less than US$1,000/ounce through 2032. C) Alternatively, Stibnite Gold Project will compete with Torex Gold's Morelos complex in Mexico, which aims to produce over 450,000 ounces of gold equivalent (“AuEq”) over the next few years and well beyond 2030. However, the AISC guidance, based on expectations for this year, is between $1,400 and $1,600 per ounce AuEq sold.

Aris Mining is conducting expansion projects and ramp-up activities in Colombia to achieve 500,000 ounces of gold production by 2026. But total AISCs were last reported in the second quarter of 2025 at US$ 1,681 per ounce of gold sold.

As in AISC's vantage point, the Stibnite Gold project, with its $435 per ounce of gold in the first 4 years or $756 per ounce of gold over the entire LOM period of 15 years, seems very well positioned relative to the operators just mentioned.

On top of the above, considering $2,900 per ounce of gold and $21.00 per pound of antimony, the Stibnite Gold Project has an after-tax net present value (“NPV”) (“at a 5% discount rate”) of $3,650 million and an after-tax net present value of $33.93 per share (with 107.57 million shares outstanding). Compared to PPTA's current NASDAQ market value of $18.21/share (at the time of writing), there is astonishing upside potential in the PPTA market, which, with an average (3-month) volume of 2.64 million shares (see Seeking Alpha in the “Trading Data” section), is anything but small for a company that is not even in the development stage yet.

Furthermore, the Stibnite Gold project has an after-tax internal rate of return of 27.1% and an after-tax payback period of 2.2 years. Based on what we've observed so far from other metal mining projects we've encountered in our analysis, 27.1% and 2.2 years have strong potential to gain market favor as the gold price rises. Regarding the sensitivity of the NPV to gold, silver and antimony prices, according to the data provided, the NPV augmented by $2,259 million, which corresponds to an increase in the price of gold of $800 per ounce, the price of silver of $4.50 per ounce and the price of antimony of $11 per pound. With a price of $3,380.87 per ounce, silver of $38.37 per ounce and antimony of approximately $55 per pound, Perpetua Resources shares could now experience impressive positive momentum as the NPV margin widens relative to the current share price, assuming the market sees the NPV of the Stibnite Gold project as a form of intrinsic value for the PPTA’s common stock.

Not to mention the potential NPV value at an average gold price of US$ 4,527.50 per ounce in 2028/2029, according to the previously presented forecasts by analysts at InvestingHaven-dot-com.

Perpetua Resources Corp.'s Stock Price Is High Relative To The Current Cycle. Investors Will Always Be Right To Buy When The Price Is At A Dip

Shares of Perpetua Resources Corp., listed on the NASDAQ under the symbol “PPTA”, were trading at $18.21 per share, representing a market capitalization of $1.95 billion at the time of writing.

Shares are trading above the MA Ribbon and are also very close to the upper end of the 52-week range of $7.61 to $18.71/share. The stock is therefore very high relative to the current share price cycle.

A number of things could happen that could hinder the project's progress towards construction as the future is uncertain, although we currently consider this adverse event unlikely. Should it happen, the shares could fall sharply from this level. There is growth potential in Perpetua Resources' future production of Stibnite gold and antimony amid strong demand for US onshore minerals. If investors are interested in the growth opportunity but do not buy shares during a downturn, there is a risk that they could lose a lot of good ground that was previously set aside, and this could happen in a short period of time if the adverse scenario for the Stibnite Gold project's progress materializes. But if investors buy during a downturn, as we always recommend, their shares are very well hedged against the downside scenario, which can never be completely ruled out when trading in the financial markets. Driven by the drop in gold prices on February 26, 2025 - the biggest drop since mid-December, as investors factored in inflation risks caused by potential new tariffs on copper and concerns about a PCE report, with the RSI at oversold levels - PPTA's share price saw a dip, and smart investors have taken advantage of these levels to reinforce their positions in Perpetua Resources.

Gold prices fell to their lowest level in nearly a month on June 27, 2025, which appeared to be more a result of investors focusing on US inflation data for clues about future interest rate developments than on easing geopolitical and trade tensions. The lower gold price sensitivity caused headwinds that also pushed shares of Perpetua Resources into a dive with the RSI close to oversold levels.

Inflation concerns have recently been a major factor in the short-lived dips in the market value of PPTA common stock on the NASDAQ, as sentiment toward nil-yielding gold bars has been dampened by muted hopes for rate cuts. The tariff war and retaliatory measures are far from over (which alone is enough to trigger renewed inflation concerns). As this scenario of tariffs and inflation-related concerns persists, bond traders may lose hope for further rate cuts following the almost certain 25 basis point rate cut at the FOMC meeting on September 17. The destabilizing impact on gold price sentiment could pave the way for another low in PPTA's share price.

Conclusion

This article upgrades Perpetua Resources Corp., a NASDAQ-listed stock with the symbol “PPTA”, from its previous “Hold” rating to a “Buy” rating as the company advances its project to build a large-scale gold and antimony trisulfide or Sb2S3 (“stibnite”) production facility at its Stibnite Gold project in Idaho.

Gold is highly valued by large investors as a hedge against turbulent global developments. Antimony is needed for various industrial applications, including the manufacture of weapons, particularly munitions, as the US lifts its military spending. The Stibnite Gold project was strategically selected by the US government because it sees potential to profitably mine this raw material on its own (relying on its domestic natural resources) and to reduce its vulnerability resulting from its dependence on imports from China. China is exploiting the US's inability to mine stibnite domestically and US's dependence on its imports through practices that do not always meet the highest standards of fairness and transparency. Perpetua Resources has raised $474 million in liquidity through equity offerings in public and private markets and is on track to secure a $2 billion debt financing from the Export-Import Bank of the United States (“EXIM”), expected in 2026. At that time, the Stibnite Gold Project in Idaho will enter the construction phase.

Rising gold prices and an antimony price that could currently be well above Perpetua's corporate presentation’s $21 per pound, are giving support to the share price. While the PPTA price rose sharply prior to the financing phase, when the company's mineral prospects appeared nearly prohibitive at best to the most, further upside is conceivable as the project's development leads to commercial production in 2028/2029.

The stock is a buy, but potential investors should further strengthen their positions during price dips, as these always represent an attractive option during volatility.

data.title: XMPT: ETF Fund-Of-Funds Exposure To Muni CEFs
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Municipal bonds provide investors with a steady and predictable stream of interest payments, with the most appealing nature that they can be federally tax-free. That translates into a higher taxable equivalent yield for investors—that is, what the yield equivalent is if an investor were holding a taxable fixed-income investment, such as corporate bonds. They also have a long history of being incredibly safe, not quite U.S. Treasury safe, but right below that.

For the tax-free nature and the safety, they pay relatively lower rates overall. That's what can make them more appropriate for investors in higher income tax brackets, who can see their taxable equivalent yields still be either comparable or respectable when put up against other fixed-income investments. Sometimes, even a lower yield can be worth it for the higher degree of safety.

There are multiple ways one can gain exposure to municipal bonds; however, today we'll be giving an updated look at the VanEck CEF Municipal Income ETF (BATS:XMPT). This fund is fairly unique in that it is a fund-of-funds, but it invests in closed-end funds.

That changes the dynamic of the fund as that introduces leverage from the underlying holdings and the discount/premium mechanic that is inherent in the structure of a CEF. At the same time, it still provides significant tax-exempt distributions to its investors. Further, it also provides massive diversification to thousands of underlying muni holdings.

XMPT Basics

  • Dividend Frequency: Monthly
  • Dividend Yield: 5.98% (SEC yield 6.21%)
  • Expense Ratio: 1.98% (including acquired fund fees and expenses)
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $168.65 million
  • Structure: Passive ETF

XMPT seeks "to replicate as closely as possible, before fees and expenses, the price and yield performance of S-Network Municipal Bond Closed-End Fund Index (CEFMXTR), which is intended to track the overall performance of the U.S.-listed closed-end funds that invest in U.S. dollar-denominated tax-exempt market."

We previously listed a breakdown of the construction of the index and, therefore, the portfolio in our previous coverage. The full methodology can also be viewed on the fund's website.

The expense ratio for this fund is quite high, but that is also because it includes the acquired funds' fees and expenses. Those are the underlying CEF expenses, which also include the leverage employed by those funds and the costs associated with those borrowings.

Results Driven by Leverage and Discount Narrowing

Since our last update early last year, the fund has performed quite strong when compared against the iShares National Muni Bond ETF (MUB). MUB and XMPT are both passively managed as they target a representation of an index. XMPT is the S-Network Municipal Bond Closed-End Fund Index, and MUB's benchmark is the ICE AMT-Free US National Municipal Index. They take a different approach, but it is a good comparison for illustrating how XMPT is doing overall.

Two factors drive the stronger overall returns during this period. It would be that the space has done well, or at least the muni space overall provided positive results. The leverage in the underlying portfolio would have boosted that.

Additionally, the fund would have seen the underlying portfolio of CEFs have their discounts narrow overall since our prior update. This was by quite a meaningful level too. The average discounts in the fund came to -15% and that's now come to sub-5%.

That helps to show the power of the opportunities that can be presented in the CEF structure with their discount/premium mechanic. ETFs don't trade wildly different from their underlying NAV per share because of the creation/redemption mechanism, keeping the share price tight to the NAV. However, for CEFs, it is based primarily on demand for the shares from investors—some funds can trade at elevated premiums for extended periods of time based on investor demand being strong for those funds.

Looking at this another way, though, while the fund has seen its underlying portfolio trade at premiums on a couple of occasions, it could suggest that upside related to discount narrowing could be more limited going forward. It appears that some investors may have taken notice as well, as AUM for this fund has fallen quite rapidly—and it wasn't necessarily the largest fund to begin with.

Interestingly, one might note a significant drop in 2022/2023. Part of that was a substantial deterioration in the share price of the fund on the back of aggressive interest rate increases from the Fed and long-term rates rising sharply. Both of those caused significant headwinds for long-duration muni assets and were amplified by the leverage within the CEFs, which also caused rising borrowing costs. We didn't see significant interest rate increases in the last year—in fact, on the short end, we saw the Fed cut 100 basis points. So this would appear to be either investors or one significant investor losing interest in the fund.

Tax-Exempt Is Great, But A Bit More Complicated

As we noted above, the TEY based on the tax-exempt nature of municipal bonds means that investors technically receive more than if they were holding the equivalent corporate fixed-income instrument. XMPT even has a nice breakdown of each of the federal tax brackets that can help to provide an illustration for investors.

However, things get a bit more complicated than what they are displaying above. They are basing that on their 30-Day SEC Yield of 6.21%. Except, the 'yield' is really that high as the distribution isn't entirely coming from income due to the underlying strategies of CEFs to attempt to reduce their discounts.

When looking at the tax breakdown of each distribution received in 2024, the total overall for the distribution paid that was tax-exempt came to 98.33%; however, this is where we need to take a closer look: the total tax-exempt interest column also including the return of capital distributions the fund received.

Technically, it is tax-exempt as it reduces an investor's cost basis in the fund. That said, it can then become taxable capital gains in the future if it is sold and an investor realizes a gain. A number of CEFs overpay their distributions to entice investors to invest in their funds, and that, in turn, generally reduces the discount the fund trades at. That isn't necessarily an automatic negative, but it is something to be aware of.

It changes the dynamic of the distribution tax classification, such as we see in XMPT, and it can also potentially lead to NAV erosion over time. That would be, unless there are corresponding unrealized or realized capital gains within the underlying portfolio that will offset the overdistribution.

There was a small portion that was listed as taxable income as a percentage of the total distribution. This wasn't a huge factor, but it is still worth considering.

Diving Deeper Into ROC's Source

The fund's largest holdings are the largest muni CEFs, that is, the Nuveen AMT-Free Quality Municipal Income Fund (NEA) and Nuveen Quality Municipal Income Fund (NAD).

Looking at NEA first, the market distribution rate of 8.01% seems quite appealing. However, the average earnings and distribution coverage come to 58.30%—meaning that the real distribution rate would come in closer to 4.67% if they paid what they were earning.

Looking at it another way, the average portfolio coupon in the fund is also earning 4.20%. That isn't too bad for munis, actually, but it still means that the NAV distribution rate of NEA at 7.85% is quite elevated.

NAD is mostly the same case as NEA, but its distribution coverage is a hair better. The 8.03% market rate is appealing, but coverage here comes in at 60.47%—with its underlying average portfolio coupon coming in at 4.40% against the 7.86% NAV rate.

This shortfall in the distribution coverage from underlying earnings derived from interest or realized capital gains is where the ROC distributions are coming from, being passed onto XMPT and then to its own shareholders.

However, the result of the higher distributions being paid by these funds has resulted in a substantial discount reduction. We already saw the average discount for XMPT go from around 15% to less than 5% since our last update. Today, these funds trade at very narrow discounts and are near the peak valuation levels we saw in late 2021.

While overdistribution can cause NAV declines over time due to destructive ROC payouts, it isn't all bad news. There are a couple of "ifs" to consider. If the Fed cuts rates more aggressively going forward, the borrowing costs will ease up on the leverage employed by funds like NEA and NAD. Lower borrowing costs mean we should likely see increased net investment income.

This is the result because interest payments go toward total investment income, and then the expenses are subtracted to arrive at net investment income. Here is a look at the breakdown of NAD and NEA as examples, looking at their last annual report for the fiscal year ended October 31, 2024.

Conclusion

XMPT provides significant diversification to municipal bonds because it takes a fund-of-funds approach. However, they invest in a pool of closed-end funds focused on muni exposure. That introduces leverage into the fund through this underlying exposure, and it also creates exposure to the discount/premium mechanism of CEFs. That mechanic inherent in the structure creates opportunities, but at this time, it is telling us there is less opportunity in terms of the discounts being rather narrow historically now. That doesn't necessarily make XMPT a terrible investment, but it certainly doesn't appear appealing for investing aggressively at this time.

data.title: Nvidia: Priced For Perfection And Still Delivering
data.description:
Introduction

Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) has just released its quarterly earnings, and boy, there was a lot to digest. The company has again surpassed analyst expectations on the back of a sustained and increasing demand for its AI GPUs. I want to take this occasion to offer a refreshed view of the company.

As to refresh readers’ memory, Nvidia is now the most valued American company as it transitioned from building computer GPUs to now offering the processing backbone of AI infrastructure, thanks to its Data Center segment and the rise of CUDA.

Q2 Earnings and Current Dynamics

Nvidia announced a beat on both its top and bottom line with a Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $1.05, a beat of 4 cents, while posting a revenue of $46.74B up over 55.6% Y/Y, and a beat of $610MM. What has surprised me has been the sheer dominance of Data Center share in its total revenue, which has also grown around 56% Y/Y. Though that was below what analysts were hoping for, as the company stated that it did not have any sales of H20 chips to Chinese customers.
