In my previous analysis, I mentioned that it will be key to see how Blackwell chips penetrate the market and Nvidia has done a great job as these have seen their sales grow by over 17%. Outside of Blackwell, Hopper architecture or the H100 and H200 GPUs are still contributing significantly to data center revenue.

I also want to touch on Gaming, which saw its revenue drastically increase this quarter with a 49% increase Y/Y or a strong 14% sequential growth. I believe that it was mainly due to the strong demand and adoption of Blackwell architecture-based GeForce RTX 50 GPUs. This momentum has legs with the similar adoption of Nvidia DLSS 4, which is the latest iteration of AI-powered super sampling technology that can significantly enhance the gaming experience on RTX GPUs.

Regarding the company’s outlook, Nvidia expects a massive $54B in sales with a 2% potential variation, though this does not include the potential H20 shipments to China. Gross margin is set to stand at 73.5%, which would be an improvement over the past 2 quarters. Now this bullish take is of course on the back of the company’s data center segment in which the demand for Blackwell GPUs has simply been “extraordinary” according to CEO Jensen Huang, and this demand has been coming from a multitude of customers such as large-scale cloud providers, sovereign initiatives and large enterprises. The Nvidia CEO has also stated that he believes that a new industrial revolution is underway and that the global AI infrastructure market could reach $3T to $4T by the end of the 2020s.

Though the company’s stock is currently down pre-market, probably due to a slight miss of revenue forecasts for the data center segment, I believe that it is more likely cherry-picking due to the heightened valuation of the company, which leaves close to 0 room for error. The company is also waiting on the actual government plan to capture 15% of the overall sales generated from H20 sales. And while waiting for this event, the company still has its regulatory licenses and doesn’t have to pay the 15% until it is official.

Regarding Visualization, the segment has generated $601MM in quarterly revenue, a healthy 32.38% Y/Y increase thanks to the adoption of the RTX Ada Lovelace architecture-based GPUs that are incredible for complex 3D simulations and AI-powered content creation. The demand for workstations with these GPUs is extremely strong as professionals keep adopting and relying on AI help for their projects. For Automotive, the segment generated a massive 69.36% Y/Y increase in its sales of $586MM, thanks to the adoption of the Nvidia Drive Orin SoC and the broader DRIVE platform used by various automakers. These two elements are now key to the future wave of software-defined vehicles that will have in-car AI cockpits and features such as advanced driver-assistance systems and autonomous driving capabilities.

Nvidia also unveiled a massive $60B stock repurchase program, which is certainly welcomed regarding the current poor state of its capital return policy to shareholders. This, on top of its record-breaking revenues, should tell us about how bullish management is about the company’s robust prospects. Though I’ve never been a fan of buying back shares at these kinds of valuations, especially now with Nvidia at all-time highs.

Valuation

Nvidia is still richly valued, as during my past analysis, with its current P/E Non-GAAP of 51.01 and its EV/EBITDA of 49.68. Though when I compare these with its peers, such as Arm and Broadcom, Nvidia makes the case for a fair valuation, considering its top-of-the-market growth as well as the biggest and most entrenched moat among all of these high-tech companies. CUDA particularly offers a killer advantage that other chip manufacturers don’t have, as it is currently the programming language of AI with over two decades of development and a massive user base. Though it is fair to say that at this current juncture, the company is priced to perfection.

This quarter has shown again that Nvidia can grow at a tremendous rate despite increasing competition and its massive size. I believe that it is thanks to the second wave of adoption, outside of cloud providers and AI companies, that reaches enterprises across many different sectors, such as finance, healthcare and retail, who are now investing massively into their own AI capabilities. Looking ahead, I believe that the company could further enhance and grow via its massive software stack, therefore diverting from pure hardware, and further enhancing the CUDA moat. Other avenues could come from the “full-stack” approach that could increase the “stickiness” of Nvidia’s platform, with for example, the Grace CPU that is supposed to work seamlessly with both Hopper and Blackwell GPUs.

It will also be interesting to see how margins will be impacted when the company ramps back up shipments to China with or without the 15% royalties to the government. Still, Nvidia has some of the best profitability metrics on the market, with its net margin exceeding 50% which is simply unheard of even in the high-tech space, while having an ROE exceeding 110%. ROIC has also reached 214.4% in the LTM, which has simply never been seen for such a large company.

Risks

Nvidia is strictly dependent on TSMC (TSM) to manufacture its products, which is a critical single point of failure, especially considering the current geopolitical tensions between China and Taiwan. Efforts by the government to bring back manufacturing to the U.S. have not yielded much either, showcasing how TSMC has become critical to the semiconductor industry. The tensions between China and the U.S. does not help Nvidia’s business either, as it is restricted from operating fully in the vast market, as well as the 15% royalties.

Nvidia is currently the dominant player in AI infrastructure, but competition is not only coming from its traditional peers, such as Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), but also from large tech companies like Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOGL) that are developing their own in-house AI chips to reduce their dependence on a single supplier.

Conculsion

Nvidia again showcased a strong quarter, leaving us shareholders wondering if anything can go wrong for the perfectly managed high-tech giant. Its Moats are still well entrenched and the world keeps on needing more and more chips from Nvidia. The company is priced to perfection, but until now, the company has done little to deviate from being perfect. I keep my Buy rating for Nvidia.

data.title: Nuvation Bio: What To Expect From IBTROZI Launch?
data.description:
Following the recent FDA approval of Ibtrozi in June this year, Nuvation Bio (NYSE:NUVB) became a commercial stage biotechnology company. Ibtrozi is an oral drug for the treatment of ROS1 positive, advanced, non-small cell lung cancer (ROS1+ NSCLC). In its second quarterly meeting, the company announced the treatment of 70 patients since the beginning of the commercialization of Ibtrozi. In this review, we will first examine the clinical trials results of Ibtrozi to see its potential for clinician adoption and review the ROS1 mutated NSCLC market to project its peak sales given its current indication. Altogether, we will see why we believe Nuvation Bio is a Buy.

Ibtrozi: A highly selective inhibitor entering the ROS1 positive NSCLC field

Ibtrozi is a next generation tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) specifically binding to the ROS1 kinase domain. Its approval was based on two Phase 2 trials: Trust-I, led in China, and Trust-II, a global clinical trial. The compilation of both clinical trials is available in the Journal of Clinical Oncology.

Both trials gathered a total of 273 patients including 93% of stage IV ROS1+ NSCLC. Among them, 160 (58%) were treatment naïve and 113 (42%) were already pre-treated with an other TKI. Overall response rate (ORR) was of 88.8% in the naïve patients and of 55% in the previously treated group. Interestingly, in patients presenting brain metastasis at baseline, the drug exhibited a strong efficacy regarding those metastasis. Indeed, intracranial ORR (IC-ORR) was of 76% in the naïve group and 65% in patients previously treated by TKI. This is of great importance since brain metastasis are one of the leading cause of death in metastatic NSCLC. This led to a median progression free survival (PFS) of 45 months in naïve and 9.7 months in pretreated patients.

Regarding the side effect associated to the drug, Ibtrozi was well tolerated with most of treatment emergent adverse events (TEAEs) to be gastro-intestinal related grade 1 or 2 side effects. Less than 10% of patients experienced neurologic TEAEs, which were grade 1.

Following those impressive results and the FDA approval for Ibtrozi, the National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) recommended the use of Ibtrozi in ROS1+ NSCLC as preferred first line treatment in its guidelines. At the light of those results, let's see if Ibtrozi is able to tackle the current established TKIs for ROS1+ NSCLC.

ROS1+ NSCLC: Current state of the market and perspective for Ibtrozi

Currently, in addition of Ibtrozi, 3 others TKI treatments are available in ROS1+ NSCLC in the first line setting: Repotrectinib, Entrectinib and Crizotinib. From real-world data, Repotrectinib is the main challenge to Ibtrozi in first line treatment. Indeed, Repotrectinib exhibited an ORR of 79% in naïve treatment patients, leading to a median PFS of 35 months. Interestingly, ORR was lower than Ibtrozi in patients already treated by TKI with an ORR of 38%, but a comparable median PFS of 9 months was reached. Finally, neurologic efficacy was also strong with an IC-ORR of 88% in naïve patients and 42% in pretreated ones.

Entrectinib also harbored promising results with an ORR of 81% and a median PFS of 12.9 months when pooling naïve and pretreated patients. Regarding its central nervous system (CNS) efficacy, the IC-ORR was 80% with an intracranial median PFS of 8.8 months.

Finally, Crizotinib is already almost outdated compared to the others. Indeed, despite an ORR of 70% and a median PFS of 24 months, Crizotinib did not report a CNS penetrance to tackle brain metastasis. Thus, to our eyes, it does not compete against the three others TKI.

Ibtrozi should in conclusion makes it place among two instituted competitors. Given the PFS results in the brain metastasis patients, the main challenger to Ibtrozi should be Repotrectinib. ROS1+ NSCLC market was valued at $290M in 2023 with a dominance of Crizotinib. However, as detailed by comparing each compounds head to head, it is expected to see this dominance decline against Repotrectinib or Ibtrozi. It is projected for Repotrectinib to reach a peak sale of $1.4B. Following this study from McKinsey, Ibtrozi, as the third to enter the market as an effective TKI for brain metastasis, would be able to take around half of this peak sale. Thus, a projected peak sale of $700M is more conservative than analysts predicting $1B. Let's now see how this would impact the valuation of the stock.

Financials and valuation

From their last quarterly report, the company holds $247M as cash in hands for a total assets of $647M. From their cash flow statement, the company used $90M in cash for the first six months of 2025. This would lead to a cash runway of around 1 year and half, which is reasonable for a company entering the commercial stage. Looking at their liabilities, the company holds a total of $274M as liabilities, leading to a stockholder equity of $372M. Compared to its current market capitalisation of $1B, this lead to a price-to-book ratio of less than 2.73, which we consider low for a company entering the commercial phase with a promising assets. Finally, applying a conservative price to peak sales multiplier of 2, the projected valuation for the company would be at least $1.5B. We believe this discrepancy is due to investors waiting to see the trend in Ibtrozi's launch and, on the longer term, see if the data from the clinical trials are confirmed in the real world setting, thus reinforcing the adoption of Ibtrozi. An hurdle that would slow the adoption of Ibtrozi would be the commercialisation which is led by the company itself, making it more difficult to compete with the others TKIs from established pharmas. However, the adoption trend seems positive from early data despite this lack of partnership. Based on the historical results of the previously approved TKI, a significant difference between clinical and real world data is not expected from our side. In conclusion, we believe Nuvation Bio to be slightly undervalued and conclude to a Buy rating.

Risk to the thesis

As mentioned, the major risk that could negatively affect the stock price of Nuvation Bio is a delay in its adoption. Given its lack of partnership for its distribution in the U.S, we could see the market share of Ibtrozi shrink against larger competitors. Also, ROS1+ NSCLC market remains untapped with other promising candidate currently in clinical phase. Positive results in those phases could negatively impact the stock price of Nuvation Bio in anticipation of a lower market share in the future.

Conclusion

Nuvation Bio holds a promising assets in the ROS1+ NSCLC field. While competitive, increasing indications for treatment and solid results obtained in clinical trials support the adoption of Ibtrozi.

data.title: The Swatch Group: Running Out Of Time
data.description:
Over the past five years, Swatch Group's (OTCPK:SWGAY)(OTCPK:SWGAF) financial performance has weakened substantially, underperforming both the broader market and its main rivals. Meanwhile, the rise of smartwatches like those offered by Apple (AAPL) have eaten into the lower-price watch segment where Swatch Group traditionally led. Its shares have correspondingly lost significant ground, delivering an abysmal total return performance.

Some of its main rivals, like LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF), which owns brands such as TAG Heuer, Zenith, and Hublot, and Richemont (OTCPK:CFRHF) which owns brands like Cartier, IWC, Jaeger-LeCoultre, and Panerai, have watches representing a relatively small part of their business. This has helped to soften the impact of weak watch demand for those peers, but even then, looking at their watch divisions they have experienced lower declines compared to Swatch Group.

Weakening Competitive Moat

Several factors have undermined Swatch Group's historic moat and brand strength, leaving it more vulnerable than ever. New competitors and technological substitutes have eroded demand, especially at the lower end of Swatch's portfolio. The clearest example is the Apple Watch and other smartwatches, which have captured a huge volume of would-be Swiss watch customers. By 2019, Apple was already outselling the entire Swiss watch industry in unit terms, and that trend continued through the early 2020s. As Forbes, explains, the Swiss watch industry was not worried because they were thinking about factors that were important in the past, failing to see the disruption threat:

In 2015, the year the Apple Watch was launched, LVMH watch division president and Tag Heuer CEO Jean-Claude Biver said the Swiss industry was not afraid of Apple’s new product, because it could not be repaired in a thousand years or eighty years, nor inherited by children, nor would it ever become a status symbol. As is always the case when disruption occurs in an industry, traditional competitors are not able to see the threat, and continue to try to analyze it according to the variables that were important yesterday.

In fact, it is clear that globally there is significantly more interest for the Apple Watch than for Swatch, based on Google Trends. There was a spike in 2022, when there was significant hype about its MoonSwatch, but the company mismanaged the rollout, leaving many users frustrated, and the hype quickly faded. The MoonSwatch collaboration in 2022, which paired the luxury Omega Speedmaster design with the low-cost Swatch format, became a global sensation and paid off in the short term, but the company has failed to replicate this temporary success.

Shifting Strategy

Strategically, Swatch Group has also doubled down on certain markets and distribution channels. With demand in China and Southeast Asia faltering lately, the company has shifted focus to other regions where it sees growth opportunities. In its second half of FY2025 results, the company mentioned a number of key markets that could deliver growth, and optimism that the situation in China is stabilizing:

The USA, Japan and India continue to have great growth potential. The Group expects a further reduction of inventories at Chinese retailers and thus a recovery in orders. E-commerce in China continues to show positive signs of increased consumption. As a result, the Group also expects production capacity utilization to improve, driven in particular by numerous new product launches in all price segments.

The U.S. in particular has grown to become a key watch market for brands such as Omega, with the company making an effort to increase its presence in new boutiques and ramping up marketing. This has increased North American sales even as Asia stalls. Still, the company now faces a new challenge with the 39% tariffs imposed on Switzerland.

Financials

Swatch's recent net profit margin of roughly 3% in 2024 is more typical of a mass-market consumer goods company than a luxury group. Swatch Group looks weak on multiple fronts, declining revenue growth, depressed operating margins, and earnings per share trending lower. Its operating margin is barely positive, and EPS is down more than 10x from their peak.

Balance Sheet

One thing that can give some hope to shareholders is that the company has a very solid balance sheet, with barely any long-term debt and a significant cash position. This should provide resources to help with a reinvention strategy, help the company survive the transition, and gives optionality to purchase strategic technology or brands.

Valuation

Swatch Group now trades at levels that some contrarian investors find attractive. The stock's Price/Earnings multiple is hard to judge given depressed earnings, but if Swatch can even partially restore growth and margins, there is significant upside potential.

Risks

One added risk is that of tariffs, as a recent Reuters article noted this will be painful for Swiss watchmakers given that they manufacture their products in Switzerland, and the U.S. had been an increasingly important market. As they cannot absorb a 39% increase they will likely raise prices, but this could result in market share losses.

Swatch Group - the biggest Swiss watchmaker by sales volume - is trying to adapt, Hayek told Reuters. The company has front-loaded shipments to the United States, is tapping U.S. shoppers as they travel abroad, and has also raised its prices. Part of the challenge for the industry is that Swiss watches need to be 60% made in the country, making it hard to shift production elsewhere.

"We produce everything in Switzerland, not in China. And we have a high cost. And when you add a 39% tariff, we cannot absorb that. So prices will go up for sure," Hayek said on Monday, without giving specifics on the potential rise.

There is also continued market share loss to smartwatches. Swatch brand's original moat, affordable Swiss-made fashion watches, has been disrupted by wearable tech. Swatch has not really launched any compelling smart wearable platform, ceding the space to the likes of Apple, and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF). At the higher end, fierce luxury competition and lagging innovation have also hurt Swatch Group's brands. For instance, Omega, long considered Rolex's main rival, has found it challenging to match Rolex's booming desirability and pricing premiums.

Another risk is continued weakness in China and Southeast Asia, where things appear to have stabilized, but have yet to fully recover. All these risks are compounded by an expensive Swiss Franc, which makes exports even more challenging.

Conclusion

Swatch still possesses enviable assets: a portfolio of storied brands covering every price segment, extensive manufacturing capability, and a rock-solid balance sheet. However, the headwinds are significant with the near-term outlook remaining challenging, and macroeconomic and competitive threats likely to persist for some time.

There is value in brands like Omega that are not reflected in today's share price due to management's missteps. However, absent a catalyst, the stock may continue to fall. Swatch Group is at a crossroads where it must evolve or continue to decline. The past five years have demonstrated that the old playbook is no longer yielding results. Further procrastination in adapting to the market could see Swatch fall irreversibly behind.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
